coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence immunoassay using triangular carbon dots for detection of the cardiac marker Troponin T in acute myocardial infarction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, So Eun
Yoon, Jae Chol
Muthurasu, Alagan
Kim, Hak Yong
coredata.dc:description: A cardiac immunosensor was developed to detect heart attacks (myocardial infarctions) at an early stage. This technique, utilizing carboxylic functionalized triangular carbon dots (caf-TCDs) combined with an antibody anti-cardiac Troponin T (anti-cTnT), proved to be a quick and sensitive method for detecting cardiac troponin T (cTnT) in both buffer solutions and biological fluids such as serum. The antibody for cardiac Troponin T (cTnT) was chemically linked to amphiphilic caf-TCDs using a carbodiimide coupling reaction. Exfoliated graphene oxide (GO) sheets effectively bind to anti-cTnT labeled caf-TCDs on their surface, resulting in a non-radiative energy transfer process where the fluorescence is suppressed. This interaction between caf-TCDs and GO follows a nonlinear and increasing Stern-Volmer relationship, indicating a combination of static and dynamic quenching. When antigen (cTnT) is present, the interaction between antibodies (anti-cTnT) and the cTnT molecules hinders the energy transfer process. This interaction also affects the positioning of the nano-couple and causes the detachment of CDs from the GO surface. As a result, the energy transfer process is impeded, leading to the restoration of fluorescence intensity. The sensor exhibits high specificity and low sensitivity to non-specific antigens. It shows a linear reaction to cTnT in the range of 0.05–50 ng mL-1, with a detection limit of 0.046 ng mL-1. Therefore, the anti-cTnT-caf-TCD/GO sensor offers a potential platform for detecting cTnT with increased sensitivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cardiac immunosensor
Triangular carbon dots
Cardiac Troponin T
Graphene oxide
And fluorescence

Synthesis of carboxylic groups functionalized T-CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government MSIT (Ministry of Science and ICT) (RS-2023-00213180).

This study was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government MSIT (Ministry of Science and ICT) (RS-2023\u201300213180). This study was also supported by the Fund of the Biomedical Research Institute, Jeonbuk National University Hospital.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of triangular CDs using hydrothermal and microwave-assisted procedures.

(A) UV–visible absorption spectra of TCDs, and caf-TCDs respectively. Photographic images of the TCDs (i&ii) and caf-TCDs (iii&iv) dispersion under normal visible light (i&iii) and upon UV light illumination (ii&iv) at a fixed wavelength of 365 nm showing their respective blue emission color. PL emission spectra of (B) TCDs, and (C) caf-TCDs respectively at various excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 420 nm.

(A) High-resolution TEM image. (A-i & A-ii) fast Fourier transfer (FFT) of the area before and after applying the mask, (A-iii) the inverse fast Fourier transfer (iFFT) of the area. Structural characterizations of the T-CDs (a) and caf-TCDs (b). (B) FT-IR spectra, (C) XRD patterns, (D) Raman spectra.
. (A) XPS survey spectra, High-resolution XPS images of (B) C 1 s, (C) O 1 s of caf-TCDs, (D) time-resolved decay profiles of caf-TCD, anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO, and anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO/cTnT.

Fluorescence studies of (A) different precursors at the nanoplatforms. (B) The fluorescence quenching spectra of anti-cTnT/caf-TCDs with increasing concentrations of GO from top to bottom. (c) The fluorescence quenching efficiency at changing concentrations of GO and inset graph Stern-Volmer plot of quenching system.

. (A) The fluorescence recovery spectra of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO system with increasing concentrations of cTnT antigen bottom to top. (B) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO system with increasing concentrations of cTnT antigen.

Cell Viability of A549 cells after 24 h of incubation with various concentrations of (A) caf-TCDs, (B) anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs, (C) confocal images for A549 cells after treatment with anti-cTnT/caf-TCDs (1 µg mL−1), and (D) confocal images for A549 cells with different concentrations (D1-D5) after treatment with caf-TCDs (1.0 mg mL−1 – 10.0 mg mL−1).
Schematic representation of cTnT based on FRET-specific interaction between anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs and GO.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence immunoassay using triangular carbon dots for detection of the cardiac marker Troponin T in acute myocardial infarction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Nano Convergence Technology, Jeonbuk National University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A cardiac immunosensor was developed to detect heart attacks (myocardial infarctions) at an early stage. This technique, utilizing carboxylic functionalized triangular carbon dots (caf-TCDs) combined with an antibody anti-cardiac Troponin T (anti-cTnT), proved to be a quick and sensitive method for detecting cardiac troponin T (cTnT) in both buffer solutions and biological fluids such as serum. The antibody for cardiac Troponin T (cTnT) was chemically linked to amphiphilic caf-TCDs using a carbodiimide coupling reaction. Exfoliated graphene oxide (GO) sheets effectively bind to anti-cTnT labeled caf-TCDs on their surface, resulting in a non-radiative energy transfer process where the fluorescence is suppressed. This interaction between caf-TCDs and GO follows a nonlinear and increasing Stern-Volmer relationship, indicating a combination of static and dynamic quenching. When antigen (cTnT) is present, the interaction between antibodies (anti-cTnT) and the cTnT molecules hinders the energy transfer process. This interaction also affects the positioning of the nano-couple and causes the detachment of CDs from the GO surface. As a result, the energy transfer process is impeded, leading to the restoration of fluorescence intensity. The sensor exhibits high specificity and low sensitivity to non-specific antigens. It shows a linear reaction to cTnT in the range of 0.05–50 ng mL-1, with a detection limit of 0.046 ng mL-1. Therefore, the anti-cTnT-caf-TCD/GO sensor offers a potential platform for detecting cTnT with increased sensitivity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is regarded as one of the primary causes of morbidity and mortality worldwide [1,2]. AMI, also known as a heart attack, happens when the blood supply to a specific area of the heart is suddenly cut off, inflicting injury or death on the heart muscle [3]. The key problem causing complexities in the early treatment of AMI is significant because it is time-sensitive [4]. Myocardial infarction is possible even when no evident symptoms are present [5]. Thus, timely and accurate evidence-based diagnosis is deemed critical for the medical treatment and management of AMI [6]. Early detection of AMI is essential in order to improve patient care by allowing for quick and effective treatment, reducing myocardial damage, and improving overall outcomes.

The most sensitive and specific biomarkers for the diagnosis and prognosis of acute coronary syndrome (ACS) are cardiac troponins (cTn), which include troponin I (cTnI) and troponin T (cTnT) [7]. Cardiac troponin (cTn) can release the bloodstream in response to cardiac injury, particularly when the myocardium (myocardium) is damaged [8]. Elevated cTn levels can be found in patients with heart failure even when there is no major blockage in the coronary arteries [9]. An elevated level of cTn, detected shortly after the start of AMI, and its sustained presence in the bloodstream for up to 10 days, establishes it as a reliable indicator for diagnosing diseases associated with AMI [10]. Nevertheless, only one-third of laboratories adhere to these assays, despite the worldwide acknowledgment of cTn as a risk marker for AMI [11]. This highlights the challenging nature of the current cTn detection methods. In general, the clinical approach is supposed to encourage shorter turnaround times while maintaining a high level of sensitivity [12]. Unfortunately, the majority of point-of-care (PoC) methods at the microfluidic or capillary level tend to be intricate and costly [13,14]. These devices require several cycles of sample loading and washing. Furthermore, many samples are required, which are acquired by an intravenous route [15]. Existing problems limit the usefulness of existing technologies and underline the need to investigate a quick, dependable, and cost-effective method for detecting cTn.

Currently, there are various techniques available for analyzing the concentrations of cTnI in blood serum [16]. These methods include enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) colorimetric procedures [17], electrochemiluminescence [18], fluorescence techniques [19], impedimetric analysis [20], electrochemical analysis [21], and chemo-resistive immunosensors [22]. Whereas these approaches offer moderate levels of sensitivity and specificity, they do have drawbacks such as being time-consuming, costly, laborious, and dependent on advanced equipment that may not be accessible in every medical facility or diagnostic laboratory [16]. When it comes to biomolecular detection, fluorescence-based detection techniques are considered to be highly accurate and reliable [23]. A particularly promising approach involves the utilization of zero-dimensional (0D) and two-dimensional (2D) nanostructures and the transfer of excited-state energy between them. Colloidal quantum dots, also known as 0D quantum dots, have been accepted for their exceptional ability to absorb and emit light with a high level of quantum efficiency [24]. Despite this, they exhibit limitations in terms of carrier transport properties. On the other hand, 2D nanomaterials such as graphene and graphene oxide have extraordinary electrical characteristics, including high charge carrier mobility [25]. As a result, the combination of 2D nanomaterials with 0D nanomaterials has the potential to lead to the development of novel sensing platforms with improved performance [26]. In this context, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is an effective method extensively utilized in biomolecular detection and various research fields [27]. For FRET-based sensors to work efficiently and effectively, a strong donor-acceptor pair is necessary. Graphitic nanostructures called carbon dots (CDs) exhibit important optical characteristics, including wide UV absorption, stable emission mainly in the green and blue regions, and up-conversion emission activated by near-infrared (NIR) light [28]. They also possess additional advantages such as being non-toxic, water-soluble, multifunctional, and easily prepared [29]. Thus, CDs have been used in a variety of bioimaging [30], cell labeling [31], and sensing applications [32]. Similarly, Graphene oxide (GO) is a type of carbon material that exists in a two-dimensional form and possesses pendant functional groups such as -COOH, -OH, and -CO, among others [33]. It displays favorable physicochemical characteristics, including excellent water dispersibility and ease of surface modification, thereby enabling the attainment of high biocompatibility [34]. Moreover, GO exhibits exceptional characteristics in terms of its structure, mechanics, thermal behavior, and electrical conductivity [33]. These distinct properties render it a highly desirable material for its potential roles as an energy acceptor in energy transfer reactions and as a constituent in photoluminescence-based sensors [35]. The aromatic segments within GO, known as sp2 domains, have the ability to effectively reduce fluorescence either through FRET or non-radiative dipole-dipole coupling [36]. This makes GO a promising option for sensing applications that require fluorescence turn-on.

In this work, we present a simple, fast, and highly responsive FRET-based immunosensor for detecting cTnT by using T-CDs and GO. A nanoprobe was created by chemically linking caf-TCDs with a capturing monoclonal antibody for cardiac Troponin T (anti-cTnT). This approach involved the covalent coupling of the anti-cTnT with the caf-TCDs via a carbodiimide coupling reaction. Further analysis was conducted to explore the fluorescence behavior of the nanoprobe when exposed to GO, which leads to the fluorescence intensity of the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs nanoprobe being quenched. In the end, the measurement of the desired antigen (cTnT) was accomplished by introducing cTnT onto a nanoprobe formed by anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO. This caused the separation of the stacked GO from the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs nanoprobe, leading to the restoration of fluorescence. As a result, this research entails an exploration of the profound optical characteristics of the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO combination and its application in detecting cTnT in both buffer solution and serum.

Phloroglucinol (PG), N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (sulfo-NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl-aminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid monohydrate (MES), disodium hydrogen orthophosphate (Na2HPO4) and sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate (NaH2PO4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, South Korea. All other chemicals used were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Bovine serum albumin (BSA), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly) and lysine (Lys), Human serum albumin (HSA), Immunoglobulin G (IgG), Myoglobin (Mb), Serine (Ser) and α-fetoprotein (AFP) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich, South Korea. Cardiac troponin T (cTnT) and troponin T antibody (anti-cTnT) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich and used as taken. Blood serum samples were obtained from Jeonbuk National University Hospital in South Korea. The chemicals that remained were of analytical reagent quality and were used just as received, without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of hydroxyl groups functionalized T-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The blue-color emitting triangular carbon dots (T-CDs) were synthesized using phloroglucinol (PG) in an ethanol solvent by a solvothermal treatment technique. Initially, 10 mL of ethanol was used to dissolve 50 mg of phloroglucinol, and the resulting solution was then transferred to an autoclave (50 mL Teflon). After that, the resorcinol solution in the autoclave was heated at 200° C for 7 h. After reaching the desired reaction time, the reactor was allowed to cool to room temperature. Filter the resulting solution through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to eliminate larger particles. Finally, dialysis of the filtrate in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da for 24 h resulted in the formation of T-CDs, which exhibited blue color emission under UV light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carboxylic groups functionalized T-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, as-synthesized functionalized hydroxyl groups T-CDs (30 mL, 1.0 mg mL-1) were completely mixed with concentrated HNO3 (8 mL), resulting in a HNO3 concentration of 3.2 M. The mixture was then microwaved in a microwave oven with a power of 240 W while heated and refluxed for 1 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the mixture was gently ultrasonicated for just a few minutes and the pH was adjusted to 8 using NaOH in an ice bath. Subsequently, the solution was filtered through a microporous membrane with a pore size of 0.22 μm to remove larger particles. The resultant solution was then further filtered via a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da, obtaining the final product of caf-TCDs.

The quantum yield is a crucial factor when assessing the fluorescence intensity of fluorescence carbon dots. It can be determined using the provided formula.
Φ =
Φ
R
×
I
I
R
×
A
R
A
×
η
2
η
R
2

where Φ is the quantum yield, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance intensity, and η is the refractive index. The reference fluorophore used in this case is quinine sulfate, which has a quantum yield of Φ = 0.54. It is dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4. The quantum yield of the aqueous solution, which contains fluorescent carbon dots and quinine sulfate, was measured by using an excitation wavelength of 350 nm (Table S1).

The caf-TCDs were labeled with anti-cTnT through a coupling process involving EDC/NHS. The caf-TCDs (50 μL, 1.0 mg mL−1) were dispersed in 1.0 mL of MES buffer (100 mM, pH 6.3) and activated by incubating EDC/NHS solution (containing 3.2 mg EDC and 1.1 mg NHS) for 15 minutes at room temperature with constant stirring. Following that, anti-cTnT at a concentration of 0.2 µg mL−1 (at 1: 1 v/v ratio) was added to the activated caf-TCDs solution. The mixture was then vigorously mixed at room temperature for approximately 1 h, resulting in the formation of the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs conjugate. The conjugate was then rinsed with PBS (0.1 M sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7.4 with 0.9 % NaCl) five times, followed by ultracentrifugation to remove any unattached molecules. Finally, the mixture was stored at 4°C in the dark.

The method for detecting target antigens (cTnT) involved observing quenching effects and then restoring fluorescence with anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs. This was accomplished by taking the following steps: A 500 µl of anti-cTnl-caf-TCDs suspension with an optimal amount of GO solution (5.5 µg mL −1) was prepared in an Eppendorf tube at room temperature to study the effect on fluorescence intensity. The changes in fluorescence intensity were observed using an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. GO was introduced at various concentrations (ranging from 0 to 5.5 µg mL −1) to quench the fluorescence emitted by anti-cTnT-caf-CDs. To validate the correlation between decreasing fluorescence intensity and GO content, a calibration curve was drawn. Following that, different concentrations of targeted antigens (cTnT) were added to a solution of anti-cTnT-caf CDs/GO (600 µL) and subsequently adjusted to a final volume of 1.0 mL using PBS with a pH of 7.4. The samples were incubated at 25 °C for 15 minutes. Afterward, the fluorescence spectra of the samples were measured with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The cTnT specificity test was carried out on human blood serum (the blood samples were obtained from a healthy individual and allowed to clot at room temperature for 5 minutes and centrifuged to obtain clear serum). Blood serum contains no blood cells, including red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), or the clotting protein fibrinogen.

The impact of caf-TCDs-conjugates on cell lines was evaluated through a microtitration (MTT) assay to measure cytotoxicity. The A549 cells were placed in a 96-well plate at a concentration of 1 × 104 cells per well and given time to adhere overnight. Next, the nanoprobe, which was based on caf-TCDs, was introduced into the well (n ≥ 6), followed by a 24-h incubation period. After that, the MTT solution (20 µL, 5 mg mL −1) was injected into each well and incubated. After a 4-hour incubation period, the medium was gently extracted from each well and the reaction was stopped by the addition of 150 μL of DMSO. The absorbance in each well at 490 nm was measured using the BMG FLUOstar Galaxy Microplate Reader. The determination of cell viability is performed by utilizing the subsequent equation: cell viability = (average absorbance of experimental wells -average absorbance of control wells with medium) × 100 % / (average absorbance of untreated wells minus average absorbance of control wells with medium).

Fluorescent images of cells were captured using a laser-scanning confocal microscope. A549 cells were placed in 6-well culture plates with a density of 105 cells per well. The cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10 % FBS and incubated for 24 hours at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 incubator. Once the DMEM was removed, a solution containing caf-TCDs at a concentration of 50 μg/mL in DMEM was introduced into each well and left to incubate for 1 h. To remove any remaining caf-TCDs outside of the cells, the cells were then rinsed twice with phosphate buffer solutions and then fixed using 4 % paraformaldehyde.

The UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu 2600 UV–vis spectrometer (Japan). The fluorescence spectra were also acquired using a PerkinElmer LS55 Fluorescence Spectrometer (US). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy is used to verify the chemical composition of the TCDs using FT-IR technology from the Varian 1000 Scimitar series. The Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope, operating at 200 kV, was utilized to capture high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. The crystallinity of the CDs was examined using X-ray diffractometry (XRD) with Cu Ka radiation (λ = 1.540 Å) from Rigaku Co. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was collected using the ESCA LAB MK-II instrument from VG Scientific Co.

The synthesis process of T-CDs is shown in Fig. 1 involves the 3-fold symmetric PG triangulogen to be subjected to solvothermal treatment at a temperature of 200 °C for 7 h. The mixture is then filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzed for 24 h. The initial substance PG triangulogen exhibits a distinct configuration in which three highly reactive hydrogen atoms are meta-positioned. These hydrogen atoms are activated by three hydroxyl groups that donate electrons within a single molecule [37]. This characteristic is crucial for the synthesis of the T-CDs (Figure S1). The resulting T-CDs were dispersed again in an HNO3 solution. The mixture was then heated and refluxed using microwave irradiation for 1 h in a microwave oven with a power of 240 W. This method resulted in the formation of carboxylic functionalized T-CDs. The synthesized caf-TCD was dispersed again in water for optical analysis and in the buffer for biological assay. Upon being subjected to UV light, the T-CDs and caf-TCDs solution displays a bright blue color emission. This indicates its excellent fluorescence efficiency and strong absorbance. Understanding the chemical composition and surface characteristics of the resulting CDs is crucial for analyzing their absorption and fluorescence characteristics. UV–visible and photoluminescence spectroscopic (PL) investigations offer insights into the optical properties of these CDs. Fig. 2A illustrates the UV–visible spectra (A) of two different types of prepared CDs, namely T-CDs and caf-TCDs. Additionally, visual representations of the resultant supernatant solutions are provided under visible (i&iii) and UV light (ii&iv) illumination. These photographs clearly demonstrate that the T-CDs and caf-TCDs emit blue light when exposed to UV light (365 nm). The UV–vis absorption spectrum shows a wide absorption range throughout the spectrum, with a shoulder peak at 245 nm and a prominent peak at approximately 280 nm. This peak might arise from the transition of electrons (π → π* and n → π*) in aromatic systems that incorporate numerous CC, and C-O/CO structures. The PL observed in T-CDs (Fig. 2B) and caf-TCDs (Fig. 2C) is dependent on excitation, as demonstrated in Fig. 2B for T-CDs at excitation wavelengths ranging from 340 nm to 420 nm. The maximum emission occurs at a wavelength of 457 nm when excited at 370 nm. Similarly, caf-TCDs exhibit the highest emission at 468 nm when excited at 370 nm (Fig. 2C). The quantum efficiencies of T-CDs and caf-TCDs are determined by comparing them to quinine sulfate as a reference standard. The quantum yields of T-CDs and caf-TCDs are measured to be 65 % and 56 % respectively (Table S1). These findings clearly demonstrate that the synthesized T-CDs exhibit a higher quantum yield.

In order to gain a better understanding of the exceptional color purity and its close relationship with the structure of T-CDs and caf-TCDs, a thorough analysis of their detailed structural characteristics was performed. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of both T-CDs (as depicted in Figure S2) and caf-TCDs (Figure S3A) reveal a highly crystalline structure resembling a triangle. The caf-TCDs exhibit an average size of 4.4 (Figure S3B). It is important to note that the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of caf-TCDs visibly depict this triangular structure with high crystallinity ( Fig. 3A). The wide-area TEM images of caf-TCDs exhibit a narrow size distribution and prominently highlight the highly crystalline triangular structure. T-CDs and caf-TCDs exhibit identical, precisely defined patterns of lattices separated by 0.21 nm, which correspond to graphene (100) planes [32,35]. The functional groups on the surface of the fluorescent CDs were identified using FTIR spectroscopy. In the FTIR spectra of T-CDs, the presence of O-H and CC functional groups can be observed through strong stretching vibration bands located at 3430 and 1638 cm−1, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 3B(a). Whereas the FT-IR spectra depicted in Fig. 3A(b) demonstrate that the samples of caf-TCDs contain a significant number of functional groups. Moreover, the intensity of the peaks at 1624 cm−1 has shown an increase, indicating the presence of carbonyl bonds and their associated CO stretching vibration, and the peak at 1516 cm−1 was designated to the stretching frequency of CC. The existence of FTIR peaks in the T-CDs and caf-TCDs indicates the existence of numerous functional groups, like hydroxyl and carboxy groups on their surface. These functional groups play a role in introducing various surface defects in the CDs, which act as traps for excitation energy and result in diverse optical properties [30]. In addition, these functional groups contributed to the high-water solubility [31]. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of T-CDs (Fig. 3C(a)) and caf-TCDs (Fig. 3C(b)) exhibit a narrow (002) peak at approximately 25°, contrasting with the wide (002) peak observed in the reported CDs [29,37]. This property indicates that T-CDs and caf-TCDs include a highly crystalline graphene structure. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 3D provides additional evidence of the high levels of graphitization. The prominent G band at 1602 cm−1 indicates well-structured crystalline graphite, while the weaker D band at 1341 cm−1 signifies disordered graphite. The ratio of the intensities of these bands (IG/ID) is roughly 1.05 for the T-CDs (Fig. 3D(a)) and 1.3 for the caf-TCDs (Fig. 3D(b)).

The results from X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigations validate the findings from FT-IR analysis and indicate that T-CDs (Figure S4) and caf-TCDs ( Fig. 4A) have identical elemental compositions, consisting of carbon (C) and oxygen (O). The XPS spectra with high-resolution show that both the C 1 s (Fig. 4B) and O 1 s (Fig. 4C) components comprise chemical bonds such as CC, CO, and O–H. The hydroxy-bonded carbon percentages in caf-TCDs decreased gradually as compared to T-CDs, as shown in Table S2. This indicates that the microwave treatment effectively resulted in carboxylic functionalization. To understand the exciton recombination dynamics in-depth, we performed measurements of time-resolved fluorescence spectra, as presented in Fig. 4D. The data shows monoexponential decay with fluorescence lifetimes of approximately 7.6 and 6.8 ns for the TCDs (Figure S5) and caf-TCDs, respectively. However, the lifetime measurements shown in Fig. 4D revealed a considerable decrease in fluorescence lifetime following the inclusion of GO. The average decay time of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO was found to be 4.8 ns. Dynamic fluorescence measurements were used again to support the detection of cTnT via FRET. The presence of cTnT increased the fluorescence lifetime of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO by 6.2 ns, verifying the detection mechanism. When cTnT was present, the tendency to regain the initial lifetime acted as a substantial barrier to the transfer of resonance energy from anti-cTnT-f-CDs to GO. The results indicate a successful regulation of energy transfer during the cTnT detection process. The decay profile of the synthesized TCDs and caf-TCDs can be attributed to the presence of various radiative states within T-CDs structures. Accurately identifying these radiative species is difficult because the fluorescence mechanism of CDs has not to be fully understood. Even so, it is believed that surface-state transitions play a role in this optical behavior. The CDs exhibit diverse functional groups like carbonyl, carboxyl, epoxy, hydroxyl, etc., on their surface states. [30] Each functional group is linked to specific energy levels. The surface states remain unpassivated. Due to the diverse range of options available, a few of them exist as non-radiative states, commonly referred to as traps. [30] Thus, the response to excitation energy by TCDs with hydroxyl groups and caf-TCDs with carboxylic groups results in the identification of decay components.

The fluorescence spectra of T-CDs during various modifications for the development of an immunosensor are shown in Fig. 5A. The original T-CDs display a strong emission peak at 457 nm. Following the acid functionalization process, the T-CDs maintained their emission peaks at 468 nm. Furthermore, when the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs conjugate was excited at 370 nm, its emission peak at 468 nm displayed an enhanced intensity, similar to that of the caf-TCDs. The retention of the emission peak in the conjugate of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs confirms the successful synthesis process. To gain a deeper insight into how the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs complex behaves under fluorescence, we introduced specific amounts of the target cTnT to the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs probe, resulting in the formation of the anti-cTnT-cafTCDs-cTnT complex. Upon the introduction of the target antigen cTnT, the position of the peak did not change significantly. However, it showed a slight decrease, which can be attributed to the specific interactions between anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs and cTnT. While the shape of the emission bands remained relatively similar, they appeared narrower in intensity and peak, indicating the formation of a complex involving anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs and cTnT. Furthermore, when GO is added to the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs nanoprobe, the intensity of the fluorescence is noticeably reduced. This is because the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs bind to the surface of GO, leading to the quenching of fluorescence (turning off the fluorescence resonance energy transfer, FRET) [33]. The GO nanosheets exhibit the ability to absorb emissive radiation across a broad spectrum of wavelengths and also demonstrate the capacity to selectively interact with specific biological entities. These attributes render them ideal for serving as a quencher. The layered structure of exfoliated GO facilitates the absorption of CDs, thanks to the distinctive morphological characteristics that enable van der Waals forces between nucleobases and the basal plane of GO [33]. There is a significant connection between CDs and GO through the involvement of delocalized p electrons and various functional groups such as carboxylic and hydroxyl groups. These interactions must occur to bring the nanostructures close together. As a result, the integration of T-CDs and GO is projected to play a crucial role in enabling effective excited state energy transfer dynamics between the two components [34]. Before applying the sensing application, an extensive analysis was conducted on the quenching performance of the nano-couple. Varying concentrations (0–5.5 μM) of GO were utilized to induce the turn-off fluorescence of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs. Fig. 5B demonstrates the fluorescence intensities at various concentrations of GO, which consistently decrease as the concentration of GO increases. Based on the concentration of GO, the efficiency of quenching improves as the concentration increases. When the concentration of GO reaches 5.5 µg mL−1, the intensity of fluorescence remains the same, indicating that the reaction between anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs and GO is complete. Therefore, 5.5 µg mL−1 is considered the optimal concentration of GO for this system. The nonlinear increase of the Stern-Volmer plot depicted in Fig. 5C demonstrates the relationship between GO concentration and fluorescence quenching [33,34]. It indicates the coexistence of static and dynamic quenching as the GO concentration rises from 0 to 5.5 μg mL−1. The close proximity of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs to the GO surface, facilitated by conjugation, leads to the observed fluorescence quenching.

In order to account for the possible use of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO in detecting cTnT by FRET (switching ‘on’), fluorescence measurements were conducted with varying concentrations of cTnT. A fluorescent probe consisting of a fixed anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO pair was employed to describe the static fluorescence response observed when cTnT is present within the concentration range of 0.05–50 ng mL−1. The pH of the medium was fixed at 7.4. It is important to note that the pH of the medium has a significant impact on the fluorescence intensity of caf-TCDs. Our findings show that caf-TCDs emit consistently within a moderate pH range, resulting in the highest amount of fluorescence intensity. Upon the introduction of the target cTnT at a concentration of 0.05 ng mL−1 to the combination of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO, the immune complex consisting of the antigen-antibody (anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO/cTnT) was formed, leading to the restoration of fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity was recovered after adding cTnT to the mixture of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO. This was observed after the formation of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO/cTnT, as illustrated in Fig. 6A. As more cTnT was subsequently added, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity occurred. This can be attributed to the formation of various complexes of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO/cTnT, which led to the expulsion of GO particles from the surface, thereby enhancing the fluorescence intensity. This complex formation caused a separation to occur between the conjugated caf-TCDs and GO, resulting in the activation of the FRET mechanism. The functioning of cTnT immunosensing is based on the distance-dependent FRET between anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs and GO. Scheme 1 illustrates the working principle of this mechanism. As mentioned earlier, anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs emit blue radiation with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. However, the fluorescence decreases in the presence of GO (5.5 µg mL −1). This occurs specifically when the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO combination is nearby. Any factor that creates distance between them disrupts the energy transfer mechanism, causing the suppressed intensity to be restored [36]. Cardiac cTnT is a large protein molecule weighing 34.6 kDa [18]. It has the ability to cause steric disruption between the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO FRET pair. When cTnT is present, the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs bind to it with high affinity through the specific interaction between the antibody and antigen (cTnT/anti-cTnT). In this scenario, significant interactions lead to the separation of caf-TCDs from GO. Consequently, the fluorescence is restored, and the change in intensity (F-F0) is directly related to the concentration of cTnT. The involvement of anti-cTnT is critical throughout this process. In the variety of biological entities present in real samples, cTnT is uniquely brought into proximity with CDs, providing it with the essential stability to interact between the donor and acceptor in this pair.

To assess the suitability of the current cTnT assay, a linear curve fitting method was employed to describe the correlation between the amplified fluorescence intensity and the cTnT concentration. A strong and consistent relationship was observed in the concentration range of 0.05–15 ng mL−1. However, deviations from this linear relationship were noticed when the cTnT concentration exceeded 15 ng mL −1. The relationship between the concentration of cTnT (ranging from 0.05–15 ng mL −1) and the resulting change in intensity can be represented by the linear equation (F-F0) = 28.7212[cTnT] + 8.87212 (Fig. 6B). The equation mentioned above was proven to be statistically significant, with a recovery percentage of 103.3 % and an accuracy level of 103.2 ± 4.0. Through regression analysis, a correlation coefficient of 0.99 has been established. The limit of detection (LOD) was also calculated, resulting in values of 0.046 ng mL −1. The values obtained from our assay were compared to those obtained from various other research-based techniques. Table S3 provides a comparison, demonstrating that our assay yields comparable or sometimes even lower values compared to the reported ones. To assess the accuracy (consistency in results) of the procedure, four samples containing cTnT at a concentration of 12 ng mL−1 in serum were analyzed on the initial day and reanalyzed after being stored for a week at 4°C using the same equipment (Table S4). A comparison was conducted between the current FRET technique's precision and ELISA, indicating a strong correlation (regression coefficient, R2=0.9988) with a calculated equation of y=0.1957x+0.04 (Figure S6). The results remained stable both within and across different batches. The coefficients of variation (% CV) observed within assays and between assays were 1.40 and 1.11, respectively, showing that the FRET approach has outstanding repeatability and reliability (Figure S5, Table S4). The conjugate of anti-cTnT/caf-TCDs displayed stability over a period of 12 months when stored at 4 °C, only experiencing a 5 % decrease in initial fluorescence intensity. T-CDs also exhibited remarkable stability, even when kept at room temperature for several months. The immunosensor's interference susceptibility was assessed by measuring the change in fluorescence intensity generated by the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO in the presence of 10 ng mL−1 of IgG, Mb, HAS, AFP, Glu, Gly, Ser, and Lys when detecting 1.0 ng mL−1 of cTnT. Various interfering substances were present in serum samples as a common occurrence. As depicted in Figure S8, minor variability was observed solely in the measurement of cTnT and the combinations (SD < 5.0 %, n=3), indicating that the functionality of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs/GO remained unaffected in the presence of interfering substances. An intriguing finding from blood serum samples (Table S5) was that cTnT showed a significant increase in comparison to the other common metal ions, which had little or no effect on fluorescence intensity (Figure S9).

The toxicity of caf-TCDs and anti-cTnT-caf-TCD pairings was assessed by exposing A549 cells to bioconjugates of CDs at a concentration of 10 mg mL−1 for a maximum of 24 h. This evaluation was carried out to determine the potential harm caused by the conjugates ( Fig. 7A). When A549 cells were treated with caf-TCD nanoprobes at a dose of 10 mg mL−1, there was no significant toxicity. Furthermore, the toxicity of the bioconjugates of anti-cTnT-caf-TCD-based nanoprobes towards anti-cTnT was investigated by incubating them for 24 h (Fig. 7B). It was observed that the anti-cTnT-caf-TCD bioconjugates, with a concentration of 100 ng mL−1, had negligible cytotoxic effects on cells. This indicates that the concentration of the anti-cTnT-caf-TCD-based nanosensors (100 ng mL−1) used for intracellular monitoring was non-harmful to cells. The original caf-TCDs demonstrate minimal cytotoxicity when the concentration is below 10 mg mL−1, attributed to their ultra-small size and high oxygen content. As a result, the T-CD-based probe that has been developed exhibits both excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, making it a promising candidate for applications in bioimaging and other biomedical fields. After examining the sensing abilities of the nanosensor in setting conditions, we continued to evaluate its efficiency in conducting molecular investigations within living cells. To accomplish this, we exposed A549 cells to a concentration of 1.0 μg mL−1 anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs and incubated them for 12 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, we used a confocal laser scanning microscope to observe the cells. As shown in Fig. 7C, we detected a substantial fluorescence emitted by the anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs after a series of washes with PBS, illustrating a successful gradual accumulation in the cytoplasm. This process is a characteristic example of passive diffusion. In contrast, an investigation was carried out into the cellular uptake of T-CDs, focusing on the relationship between concentration and uptake to better understand the underlying mechanism. The results shown in Fig. 7D (D1-D5) illustrate that as the concentration of T-CDs increased, the uptake by A549 cells showed enhanced acceleration. This observation indicates the involvement of energy-dependent active endocytosis is involved in this mechanism. Thus, it can be inferred that the cellular uptake of T-CDs involves both energy-dependent endocytosis and passive diffusion, similar to numerous other types of nanoparticles. These findings demonstrate that our T-CDs have the potential to be a highly efficient luminescent probe for bioimaging applications as well as an excellent Troponin T-sensing probe in laboratory settings.

In this work, we have presented a sensing platform consisting of triangular carbon dots (T-CDs) and GO for the specific detection of cardiac troponin T. The optical investigation successfully revealed an efficient transfer of dynamic energy from carbon dots to the two-dimensional nanosheets of GO. The measurements of fluorescence in this study provide insights into a sensing platform that develops a combination of anti-cTnT-caf-TCDs and GO nanoparticles. The sensor shows a linear response to concentrations of cardiac troponin T ranging from 0.05 to 50 ng mL−1, with a detection limit of 0.046 ng mL−1. The fabricated sensor exhibits negligible response towards other proteins and biomolecules that are commonly found in high concentrations in blood serum, surpassing the level of troponin T. Consequently, this method demonstrates significant promise for application in the analysis of real samples. Thus, by altering the surface characteristics of carbon dots, it is possible to enhance the fluorescence capabilities of the probe and improve its sensing performance. In essence, this study suggestfs that there are potential opportunities for the detection of cardiac troponins thanks to these material modifications.
So Eun Kim: Writing – original draft. Jae Chol Yoon: Investigation. Muthurasu A: Writing – review & editing. Hak Yong Kim: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing for financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government MSIT (Ministry of Science and ICT) (RS-2023–00213180). This study was also supported by the Fund of the Biomedical Research Institute, Jeonbuk National University Hospital.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.136368.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: So Eun Kim: Currently serving as a Research Faculty in the Clinical Medicine of Jeonbuk National University and Biomedical Research Institute of Jeonbuk National University Hospital, Jeonbuk National Univerisity. She received his Ph.D. degree in Bionanosystem Engineering from the Jeonbuk National University. Her primary research interests are focused on the development of novel nanostructured materials and their applications for bio-medical. Other interests are working on designing biomaterials and drug delivery platforms for biomedical applications.

Jae Chol Yoon: Currently serving as a Research Faculty in the Clinical Medicine of Jeonbuk National University and Biomedical Research Institute of Jeonbuk National University Hospital, Jeonbuk National Univerisity. He received his Ph.D. degree in Bionanosystem Engineering from the Jeonbuk National University. His primary research interests are focused on the development of novel nanostructured materials and their applications for bio-medical. Other interests are working on designing biomaterials and drug delivery platforms for biomedical applications.

Alagan Muthurasu: obtained his BSc and MSc degrees in general chemistry from The American College – Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India. He then received his Ph.D. degree in 2017 from the Central Electrochemical Research Institute (CSIR-CECRI) in Karaikudi, India. Currently, he is working as a Research Faculty at the Department of Nano Convergence Engineering, Jeonbuk National University, South Korea. His research focuses on the synthesis and design of durable non-precious metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) derived hybrid materials for electrocatalytic water splitting, metal-air batteries, sensors, and supercapacitors. Other interests are working on designing biomaterials and drug delivery platforms for biomedical applications.

Hak Yong Kim: was awarded his BS degree from Jeonbuk National University (JBNU) and MS and PhD degrees from Seoul National University. He was appointed the leader of the Samyang Group R&D center in 1984 and professor of JBNU in 1998. He is currently the Director of the Nano-convergence Institute for Innovative Energy Materials and Devices and a distinguished professor in organic materials and fiber engineering. His research interests focus on inorganic and organic nanomaterials in various fields like energy, environment, etc.
